sentences.text: Soldiers, yes.
So we disappeared next day.
And there was the-- if they would have behaved-- well, now I couldn't blame them, either.
They were hungry, you know, men.
We were not hungry girls.
But we were very naive-- really very naive, because what I hear from other people, you know, things, we didn't know nothing.
That's how what we know just to survive.
And tomorrow, we go home.
That was our-- our idea.
That what kept us going.
But, you know, while we walked, it was-- it wasn't just bad for us.
It was for all the people very bad.
For the Russian, too.
They were not better off from me, because it was-- everybody was-- was bad.
You know, they took away all the men.
It was just the woman, and there was a lot of fields.
You know, the German took away the-- whatever-- they-- they cleaned up everything.
And there was a lot of hunger.
It was bad.
And then even the Russian-- the few, they used to steal a lot.
So it was panic.
It was all the time was that panic.
And that-- you know, we-- we accumulate some wheat, and we made some flour.
And we baked it in a stove, because there was no-- and that was a very bad end when the Germans-- when they pushed away the Germans.
And we said we're going to-- we're going to take a long time till you could come home, so we should have what to eat.
And we baked that bread, and we kept it.
And we were afraid to eat the extra bite.
And then we had to-- we stayed maybe two or three weeks in one station to-- till we could get to go somewhere else.
You know, you couldn't walk.
You had to have a train.
But not on every train you could jump up.
It took a couple weeks till we could jump on a train.
In meantime, we give the bread in a storage.
It all got spoiled.
We didn't realize.
So we worked so hard, and we-- we were afraid to eat it.
[LAUGHS] But we made it.
Like I said, we made it.
And we could have lived different, too, because you met-- the Russian soldiers were very nice.
Some of them were very nice.
One of them met, and he said he want to marry me.
He said, you know, you're going to go.
I send you to my parents.
And he was from south of Russia.
And then I come back, we-- we going to have a nice life.
And there was a storm outside, too.
So I said, look it, I would marry you, but I have two more.
So what could I do?
So he was thinking.
And he went to his officer, and he talked.
And he said, it's all right.
He going to make papers for all three of us to go.
So while he was debating with his officer, we took off.
We run away.
Who want to go with the-- you know.
We could have had opportunities, but like I said, we were very naive.
And we were afraid for everything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
But that was a rough time.
It was a rough time.
We met a lot of dead people on the way.
You know, left over.
Like, the Germans had a lot of-- from Czechoslovakia, they had a lot Italian soldiers.
And they were laying, you know, half-dead.
They must have died, because nobody picked them up.
What could we help them?
One solider give us the address for his parents to write them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But like I said, we-- we made a-- a diary.
I was writing in Blasberg, and she lost it after the war.
That diary-- we would have had-- you could have had, like, the best movie.
It was everything-- every little piece what happened.
Three times a day-- one day I was writing, one time she was writing.
And we treasured it so much.
It was-- you know, we going to show our parents what happened.
And we came back, and she kept it.
And then she give the one who was in Israel, and she lost it [INAUDIBLE] the evidence.
No.
We were not sick, no.
But she had malaria, but I was never sick.
I was never sick.
And the other one was never sick, neither.
No, not there.
Not in the-- that part of Russia.
No.
I worked on the-- in one village I was, I used to work on the combine.
You know what a combine is?
You take down the wheat from the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we did everything just to survive.
They used to raise pigs.
I wasn't good to raise pigs.
You know, you get dirty pigs.
You have to feed them and put them in their-- wherever they stayed.
So I wasn't too good in that.
So I used to drive a combine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went-- took a couple months till I learned.
So in meantime, you know, I had a little food and I was warm.
We did a lot of-- a lot of things.
Did you kind of teach yourself as you went?
Or did somebody show you what to do?
Well, the mechanical-- I mean, you have to learn.
Where I come from, we didn't have this machinery or nothing.
No.
If people that give up hope, we would not survived.
That was our-- that was our hope.
You know, we go home.
The parents are waiting.
No.
They used to announce, but not the-- the true.
You know, the Russian, they used to have a-- a speaker in middle of that-- of the little village.
And it used to say what it is.
But most they used to say from-- from the front what the-- the Germans did, what the Russian did.
But not what happened to people. .
No.
No.
No.
That's why we had hope.
Well, help-- we worked together.
We eat together.
We slept together.
We slept in a room with 30 people in the room, so you have to live-- be nice.
Otherwise, you could not exist.
It was impossible.
And there were people from all over Russia.
We were the only one from the-- from Poland.
But the rest was from different part-- part of Russia.
So we had to get along.
No, no.
That was no-- they didn't care that time.
Everybody cared just to survive.
If you had a little food and wood to sleep, that was-- that's all.
You didn't need nothing no more.
That's right.
Well, when-- when I knew the Germans were in-- behind the old border.
Not my town.
In general, there was the border between Poland and Russia.
See, Russia took over half Poland, but it used to be-- before, it was a border between-- before the Russian took over.
So when the Russian took over the part of Poland where they occupied in 1941, that we considered our territory.
So we start to make plans to go home.
The-- that's right.
That's-- no.
My hometown was not a part of it.
No.
But we didn't care.
We thought whatever happens, so long as we are past the old border, we are home.
It's going to be different.
We didn't want to be in Russia, neither.
We didn't know what's happened, but, you know, that was our plans.
So we start to make plans.
And that took maybe two months till we could get a ride on the train.
I told you what we used to do.
We had a bottle of whiskey.
Used to show them, then we come in, we disappear.
And, you know, there was commotion.
Nobody cared no more.
But so long they saw, they thought we going to give them right away.
But so we go-- we went in.
So that took a couple months till--
What?
That was in 1944.
And then, you know, we stayed in villages, too.
Even on the-- before the border-- right on the border.
And one time we went in, and a lady let us in, all three.
And it was Easter.
And she-- yeah, before we came to that town, we walked, because the train didn't go.
And we were sitting.
We were tired from walking, and we sat on the highway.
And they came, and they used to call them [RUSSIAN].
You know, the Russian hooligans who used to rape people and kill people.
But we didn't know.
And they did something, and the police were after them.
And they were running, and here we were sitting.
There were maybe 10 of them.
So they-- they stopped, and they said, who are you?
And we didn't say we are Jews.
We said we are Polish people.
So they start to swear on us.
They don't like the Polish people, neither.
And he took away mine shoes.
And so the girl said, see?
You-- you didn't want to wear them.
It's so cold now.
I said, thank god they took the shoes.
They could have killed us.
And then the police came and said, my, god.
They didn't kill you?
They kill everybody.
So we were spared, not knowing what it is.
So we went to that village, and it was Easter.
And the lady-- lady let us in.
And we said we are Ukrainian.
You know, we knew not to say Polish.
And then, you know, they go to church middle of the night.
So the one who's in Israel didn't want go to church, so I went with the other one to church.
You know, you have to-- and I didn't know how.
So you look, and you mimic, just not to show who we really were, because you're-- they knew who we-- they would have not let us in.
They didn't like the Jews, neither.
So we stayed there for two days.
We went to church and every-- they were Greek Orthodox.
And she'd give us something to eat.
And then we went to a different village.
And you couldn't cross the old border, because this Ukrainians used to kill people very much.
So we had to wait.
So then the soldiers-- the Russian soldiers-- took some people on the trucks in middle, and the soldiers were on the sides with the rifles.
And that's the way we crossed the border.
You couldn't walk.
It was very dangerous.
And Then we come-- we came to Rowne.
Mine city was Lemberg, come-- wherever I originally come was not far.
But Lemberg was still by the Germans.
So we came to Rowe, and we came in in the office.
And we were the first people who came.
They didn't let nobody go yet.
So he said, what could I do with you?
And I show him mine paper, and he said, what could you work?
Would you like to work in a grocery store?
And I said, no.
Because, see, if you make more a penny or less the penny, you get punished.
You-- you have to be exact.
So I knew from before the war that-- I said, no, that's too much responsibility.
So he said, well, you could be a teacher.
So they give me a teacher's position.
And the other one had a teacher's position.
The other two were working in the hospital.
So we had a little room they give us in the school.
And we-- we made a little money.
We could just have one meal.
You know, that's how much we made.
And that where I met my husband in Rowne.
See, he was from a different city.
But there was one week where the Russian put back the Germans, and then the Germans took back over.
In that week, whoever survived-- a few Jewish people who survived-- ran to Rowne, because it was liberated.
So he lived across the street, and he worked for the Russian army.
You know, that's the only way you could survive.
They're given papers, and you work for the army, not in the front, but behind the front.
And then the Germans used to come every night and bombard terrible.
In the school we were in, there was a place where you could hide.
And the Germans knew about it.
So every night, the whole town used to go in the fields, because a lot of people got killed when the-- when the shelters.
And one night we didn't went to the shelters, and they came to bombard.
And we went to that shelter-- three of us.
Everybody went away.
We didn't go.
And my husband knew we were there.
And, you know, in middle bombarding, he knew.
And he came to us.
It was terrible.
They bombard the whole shelter around, but they didn't-- I remember, you know, he came, and we-- you know, hold him.
All three of us hold him.
And it was a such a power.
You know, where-- everybody was flying on the-- from the-- from the-- from the bombs.
We were up and down up-- it was terrible.
It was a lot of dead people next day there.
So he lost his wife and a child and the whole family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were going every night.
You know, you get acquainted.
And I was so stupid, and then he said, let's get married, you know, after a while.
And, you know, I used to call him by the second name maybe two years after I got married.
I couldn't-- I was ashamed to call him but his first name.
It was funny.
And I told him, I said, look it, I don't know how to cook.
I don't know-- I'm no good.
He said, you don't have to.
So all the years I used to joke.
I said, I didn't promise you nothing.
[LAUGHS] It was true.
So after I got married-- a month after I got married-- they liberated Lemberg.
When did you get married?
In the begin.
In July, I think.
In July.
I got married.
You know, he's give us some money.
We went on the market and bought a few things.
And then he called his friends, you know.
But after a month I got married, they liberated Lemberg.
So I was married, and they said they're going to go-- they're going to go back.
The two girls.
But you couldn't just go.
You have to hitchhike.
You know, not on the train, but you have to hitchhike on trucks, you know, from the-- with the soldiers.
And I said, I want to go, too.
But my husband knew there's nobody.
But we wouldn't believe it.
Well, finally I-- you know, he begged me.
He begged me.
And I felt bad.
He said, look at to me.
Let them go.
And he give them plenty money.
If there is somebody alive in you family, they will have money to give him and then they will write us.
And we both going to manage somehow to go.
So they went.
It took maybe a month, which you could go from Lemberg to-- to Rowne.
It was maybe a couple hours.
It took them almost a month to hitchhike.
Not everybody wanted to take you on the truck, and not with everybody you would go.
So they came to the city where I was born, because their parents were there, too.
And they saw nobody was there.
It was a heap of-- of stones.
So they tell them the next city, [INAUDIBLE], there are a few Jews who hid in the bunkers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they went.
At that time, they still could walk.
It was maybe 20 kilometers.
It took two-- a couple good hours to walk there.
So they went there.
And one of the-- the one who in Israel find three cousins of her survived.
And the Blasberg's husband was with them.
And they hid in bunkers there.
They knew some Gentile people, you know.
And they hid.
And in the night time, they used to go steal some food.
And they survived.
But then they wrote me a letter.
But it took three months till I got the letter.
In meantime, I got itchy, you know.
And my husband said, look it, if there would be somebody alive, we would have known.
But I said, how could you know?
How could you know?
And then I said, what did I did to myself?
Why did I get married?
If mine parents is alive-- see, my husband was 20 years older from me.
I said, what did I did to myself?
You know, I was-- and I cried, and I cried.
So he said, OK.
Do whatever.
He couldn't take it.
He said, do whatever you want.
So I hitchhiked.
It took me two weeks to hitchhike to Lemberg.
So when I came to the town where I come from, I saw nothing.
And they told me where they are, but I couldn't walk no more.
You couldn't go there, because it was very dangerous.
You know, the Ukrainians were very-- they murdered people right and left.
Even the Russian soldiers were afraid for them.
And I saw I don't have nobody.
And they told me what happened.
So what could you do?
Then I met a Gentile boy who used to be a friend, you know.
We were playing together when we were home, maybe did things together.
His parents died, and he survived with a little sister.
But the sister was some-- in a little village behind that city.
And he said, let's stick together, and I go with you.
And then we met another guy who was born in the United States.
His parents were in the United States.
And then the mother was in a big accident, and she got a lot of money.
She came back and bought a beautiful home, and they lived in that town.
But he was born in the United States.
And he said to me, you know what?
He said, I go with you whatever happen.
And if I come, if something going to be quiet, I will write-- he must've known where he was born-- and I am a citizen of the United States.
So we were waiting and waiting to pass-- a truck should pass, because you have to hitchhike.
And I was sitting.
And then from the Orthodox Church, the priest came.
And he know me.
And he express his condolences, you know.
Said, nobody.
And if he could help me, something.
I said, why do you want to help me?
You didn't help nobody.
There-- not one person survived from that town.
Not one person.
From-- from your hometown?
Magierow?
From my home-- yes.
Not one person.
There were a few, but they were in Russia, not from-- in the meantime, he said he going to pick up his little sister, and we're going to go together.
And I said, fine.
In meantime, a truck came with soldiers.
And I went in.
I said, would you-- would you take me to Lemberg?
He said he doesn't go to Lemberg.
He goes to a city between Lemberg.
But he'd gladly give me a ride.
And I thought to myself, my God.
I was just married, and I come at a handsome Gentile boy.
He was talking Jewish.
He always associated with Jews more like Christians.
I said, what-- what is my husband-- you know, thought.
And then the truck driver says, well, if you want to go, come.
So I hopped on the truck, and he didn't even know what happened to me.
I felt bad later, you know.
Later I felt bad, but in meantime, I thought-- so then he let me out to that city.
But then from that city to go to Lemberg was very dangerous, too.
But I couldn't get a truck.
And, you know, I walked, and nothing happened to me, which everybody was admiring me.
How did I have the guts to walk even?
And then to Lemberg, I had to hitchhike.
It took me maybe two weeks to-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
It took me two weeks to come home.
Well, I came home with whatever I went.
Nobody was there.
But then it came out all the people who were Polish citizen, if they don't want stay in Russia, they could leave and go to Poland.
So then the Jewish Zionist organization was organized.
So we registered, and we went to Poland.
In meantime, I had a baby.
You know, my older son was born in May in-- in April, 1945.
So that's how we-- we came to Lodz.
And we stayed there maybe for six months.
And then we went to Austria.
And from Austria, some went to-- some went to Germany.
Whoever want to go to America, went to Germany.
Whoever want to go to Israel, went-- went to-- to Italy.
So from Austria, we went to Yugoslavia.
And from Yugoslavia, we went to Italy.
And then we lived there for a couple of years till we-- and then we-- Let's stop right here, and we'll pick it up here on the next tape, OK?
